coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots based intelligent sensing platform for the detection of alizarin red in beverages and vegetables
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Fu, Xueyu
Zheng, Min
coredata.dc:description: Alizarin red (AR), a strong oxidant, is a chemically stable and non-biodegradable dye, which might be a significant threat to environment and public health. However, accurate detection of AR continues to face an enormous challenge owing to the absence of highly efficient and sensitive detection approaches. Herein, blue luminescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized, which had remarkable sensitivity and selectivity toward AR. The linear range when using the fluorescence/spectrophotometer is 10–100 μM, and the LODs are 5.90 μM and 0.53 μM respectively. Besides, when CDs were mixed with AR, the color changed from light yellow to pink, and the nuanced shifts in color might be identified with the naked eyes via colorimetric mode with the help of smartphone RGB app. This portable detection platform kept the merits of good operability, fast response and high accuracy, the linear range was from 10 to 100 μM, with an LOD of 0.05 μM. By virtue of the superior performance, CDs based nanosensors were successfully utilized for the determination of AR in drinks and vegetables. The work provides a new strategy of constructing fluorometric/spectrophotometric/colorimetric trimodal sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots (CDs)
Alizarin red (AR)
Fluorometric
Spectrophotometric
Colorimetric sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference of AR detection
Detection of AR in real samples
The selectivity and anti-interference of CDs
Detecting AR in the real samples
Detection of AR in vegetable and beverages with a smartphone platform

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of CDs. (B) Size distribution of CDs in (A). (C) XRD pattern of CDs. (D) FT-IR spectrum of CDs. (E) XPS spectra of CDs. (F) HR scans of the C1s, N1s, and O1s regions are presented in (G), (H), and (I), respectively.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs. (B) Fluorescence spectra of CDs. (C) Fluorometric titration curves of CDs with AR. (G) Linear fitting curve of the intensity at 395 nm vs [AR]. (D) Spectrophotometric titration curves of CDs with AR. (E) Linear fitting curve of the absorbance at 515 nm vs [AR].

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, AR, CDs + AR. (B) UV–vis absorption spectrum of AR and fluorescence spectrum of CDs. (C) Fluorescence decay curves of CDs, CDs + AR. (D) Scheme illustrating the mechanism by which CDs detect AR.
(A) Photos of CDs mixed with different concentration of AR solution under daylight. (B) The R/G value of CDs vs the concentration of AR.

(A) Photos of beverage containing CDs mixed with different concentration of AR solution (0–100 μM) under daylight. (B) The R/G value of beverage containing CDs vs the concentration of AR. (C) Photos of flammulina mushrooms soaked in CDs and AR solution (0–300 μM) under daylight. (D) The R/G value of flammulina mushrooms vs the concentration of AR.
(A) The preparation of CDs. (B) Detection of AR through the fluorescence of CDs and (C) the detection of AR through three modes.
The results of CDs detecting AR in genuine water samples.
Comparison of various detection methods of AR.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots based intelligent sensing platform for the detection of alizarin red in beverages and vegetables

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130012, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Advanced Institute of Materials Science, Changchun University of Technology, 2055 Yanan Street, Changchun, Jilin 130012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Alizarin red (AR), a strong oxidant, is a chemically stable and non-biodegradable dye, which might be a significant threat to environment and public health. However, accurate detection of AR continues to face an enormous challenge owing to the absence of highly efficient and sensitive detection approaches. Herein, blue luminescent carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized, which had remarkable sensitivity and selectivity toward AR. The linear range when using the fluorescence/spectrophotometer is 10–100 μM, and the LODs are 5.90 μM and 0.53 μM respectively. Besides, when CDs were mixed with AR, the color changed from light yellow to pink, and the nuanced shifts in color might be identified with the naked eyes via colorimetric mode with the help of smartphone RGB app. This portable detection platform kept the merits of good operability, fast response and high accuracy, the linear range was from 10 to 100 μM, with an LOD of 0.05 μM. By virtue of the superior performance, CDs based nanosensors were successfully utilized for the determination of AR in drinks and vegetables. The work provides a new strategy of constructing fluorometric/spectrophotometric/colorimetric trimodal sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Alizarin Red (AR), an anthraquinone compound known for its high color fastness, is widely used in the textile industry as a dye [1,2]. Recognized for its toxic effects, AR is also a well-documented organic contaminant in wastewater. The complexity of its fused aromatic structure renders AR challenging to break down, consequently representing a significant threat to environmental and human health due to its substantial emissions. Therefore, the development of effective AR detection methods is imperative. So far, various techniques for detecting AR have been introduced, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [3,4] and electrochemical assays [5,6]. Despite their accuracy, conventional techniques such as HPLC and electrochemical assays suffer from inherent drawbacks, including multi-step sample preparation, reliance on expensive instrumentation, and limited field adaptability. For instance, HPLC analysis of AR typically requires 20–30 min per run [3], while electrochemical sensors demand frequent electrode replacement due to fouling [6]. These constraints highlight the urgent need for low-cost, user-friendly alternatives capable of real-time monitoring. Regrettably, all the cases we are aware of involve the detection of AR using fluorescence methods. In the known cases, both MXene quantum dots and tongue nanoclusters are synthesized under stringent conditions through elaborate, multi-step processes [7,8]. This clearly highlights the necessity of developing user-friendly probes with fluorescence and other properties, which can conveniently and efficiently detect AR.

Carbon dots (CDs), a novel category of fluorescence material, have garnered wide-ranging applications across sensing [9–13], biological imaging [14–18], and disease diagnosis and treatment [19–23], attributed to their exceptional optical characteristics, biocompatibility, eco-friendliness, affordability, and aqueous solubility [24–27]. CDs, in particular, excel as nanoprobes for environmental pollutants (like heavy metals, antibiotics, and organics) [28–36], as well as biomolecules (such as pH indicators, DNA, nitrites, glucose, and biothiols) [37–46], by tracking changes in their fluorescence. As evidenced by Yang et al. reported the creation of N and Cl co-doped CDs, which demonstrate the capability to discern tartrazine in soft drinks [47]. Zhong et al. developed CDs to detect the chemotherapy drug mitoxantrone in blood serum [13]. Despite these advancements, the use of CDs for AR detection remains unexplored. Moreover, relying solely on one type of detection can compromise result accuracy. Therefore, it is essential to innovate multimodal CDs-based probes to detect AR, with an emphasis on both convenience and reliability.

In our research, we utilized L-arginine (L-Arg) and pectin as the sources of nitrogen and carbon, respectively, to synthesize CDs through a pyrolytic method as depicted in Scheme 1 . Incorporating AR led to the fluorescence quenching of CDs, and a color change in the solution from light yellow to deep pink. Consequently, CDs demonstrated the capability for selective AR detection through a trimodal approach fluorometric, spectrophotometric, and colorimetric, offering rapid response and precise quantification. Utilizing spectroscopy and a smartphone-based color recognition application, the CDs were effectively employed for AR quantification in authentic samples and in needle mushrooms. This research introduces a sturdy and dependable detection platform designed for the ultra-sensitive determination of AR.

Notably, single-mode detection methods often face trade-offs between sensitivity, operational complexity, and field applicability. For example, while fluorescence offers high sensitivity, it may require specialized equipment; spectrophotometry provides quantitative accuracy but lacks portability; colorimetric assays enable visual screening yet suffer from limited precision. By integrating these three modes, our CDs-based platform combines their individual advantages: (1) fluorescence ensures ultra-sensitive quantification (LOD: 5.9 μM), (2) spectrophotometry validates results with spectral specificity (LOD: 0.53 μM), and (3) colorimetry allows rapid on-site screening via smartphone (LOD: 0.05 μM). This multimodal design not only cross-verifies detection reliability but also accommodates diverse scenarios from laboratory analysis to field testing addressing the limitations of conventional single-approach methods.

A mixture of L-arginine (174 mg) and pectin (150 mg) was solubilized in 20 mL of high-purity water, resulting in a uniform solution. This liquid was transferred to a round-bottomed flask and heated in an oil bath set to 180 °C for 60 min. Post-heating, the concoction was left to return to room temperature, followed by the addition of 3 mL distilled water to ensure complete dissolution of the substance.The crude material was subsequently enclosed within a dialysis pouch with a 500 Da cutoff and dialyzed for a duration of 12 h. The process concluded with freeze-drying the dialyzed CDs, leading to a final yield of 47 %.

At room temperature, different amounts of the AR solution (1.5 mL) were added to the CDs solution to maintain a constant CDs (1.5 mL) concentration at 200 μg/mL, with AR varying between 1.0 and 500.0 μM. Subsequently, the blend was transferred into a cuvette fashioned from quartz with four chambers. Utilizing a spectrophotometer set to an excitation of 320 nm, the emission spectra were captured, reflecting the fluorescence properties. An inverse relationship was noted between fluorescence intensity and AR levels, culminating in extensive fluorescence suppression at the peak concentrations of AR. To confirm precision, every measurement was performed three times. LOD was determined from the Stern-Volmer plot.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference of AR detection
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of CDs towards AR, individual solutions containing 100.0 μM of various potential interferents—namely AR, Cu(NO3)2, FeCl2, NaF, NaCl, Cd(NO3)2, NaNO3, KCl, L-histidine (L-His), L-proline (L-Pro), glutamic acid (Glu), L-phenylalanine (L-Phe), L-leucine (L-Leu), glutathione (GSH), dopamine (DA), urea, and melamine were each blended with a CDs solution set at 200.0 μg/mL. Fluorescence spectra under 320 nm excitation were then obtained to assess the interaction between CDs and AR in the presence of each interferent. With the CDs and AR concentrations held steady at 200.0 μg/mL and 100.0 μM, respectively, fluorescence recordings were taken (at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm and emission at 395 nm), allowing for the assessment of potential fluorescent interference from the various compounds.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AR in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: AR, a common environmental contaminant, poses a significant threat to water resources. In our study, a diverse range of real-world samples were selected to investigate AR contamination, including tap water, grapefruit juice, commercial beverages, vitamin-fortified water, and the vegetable flammulina velutipes. For liquid samples, each was first centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min, followed by purification using a 0.22 μm membrane to remove insoluble particles. Then, varying concentrations of AR were added to these samples to simulate potential contamination scenarios. By measuring the fluorescence spectra, we were able to determine the AR recovery rates in these liquid samples using a pre-determined linear equation. Regarding the solid sample, flammulina velutipes, it was first cut into long strips approximately 2 cm in length. These strips were then immersed in a 200 μg/mL CDs solution for 2 h. Subsequently, the treated flammulina velutipes strips were soaked in an AR solution with concentrations ranging from 0 to 300 μM and incubated for 12 h. Finally, the content of AR in the sample was determined using a linear equation based on the color change in RGB values.

CDs were produced through the pyrolysis of L-arginine (L-Arg) and pectin at a temperature of 180 °C. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images reveal that the CDs are uniform globular particles with a mean diameter of 3.75 ± 1.37 nm (Fig. 1 A). X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis revealed a distinct characteristic peak at 20° (Fig. 1C), corresponding to the graphite (002) crystal plane. This peak position shift (compared to the standard graphite peak at 26.5°) is likely attributed to the expansion of interlayer spacing caused by heteroatom doping (N, O) and surface functional group modification (–OH, –COOH) [48]. Functional group characterization of CDs was conducted via Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The FT-IR spectrum, depicted in Fig. 1D, displays prominent peaks appear at 3390 and 3180 cm−1, indicative of the O-H and N-H bond stretch vibrations, respectively. Vibrational bands located at 2920, 2850, 1640, 1410, 1240, and 1100 cm−1 correspond to the C-H, –CH2, C=O, C=C, C-O, and C-N groups. The abundance of hydrophilic groups enhances the aqueous solubility and stability of CDs, facilitating the creation of sensors that utilize CDs. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) provided further insights into the chemical composition of CDs. The comprehensive XPS spectrum, illustrated in Fig. 1E-1F, shows peaks with binding energy of 284.6, 399.1, and 532.9 eV, aligning with carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, accounting for 43.34 %, 8.14 %, and 48.53 % of the composition respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum identifies bands at 284.6, 286.0, and 288.1 eV, representing C-C or C=C, C-O or C-N, and C=O functionalities (Fig. 1G). Additionally, the high-resolution N1s spectrum features two peaks at 399.8 and 400.5 eV, indicative of C-N and N-H bonds (Fig. 1H). In the high-resolution O1s spectrum, peaks at 531.57 and 532.54 eV are noticed, corresponding to C=O and C-O linkages (Fig. 1I).

To assess optical properties of CDs, we conducted UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectroscopy measurements. The UV–vis absorbance of CDs presented two marked peaks at 215, 302 nm (Fig. 2 A), attributable to the π-π* transition in C=C and the n-π* transitions in C=O/C-N bonds, respectively. When the excitation wavelength was varied from 300 to 380 nm, a corresponding shift in emission maxima from 380 to 440 nm was noted (Fig. 2B), with the most intense emission observed at 395 nm under a 320 nm excitation. Additionally, the ionic interference resistance of CDs in NaCl solution was investigated by progressively increasing NaCl concentration up to 600 μM, which revealed negligible effects on the fluorescence of CDs (Fig. S1A). This confirms the non-interfering nature of NaCl on CDs emission. Exposure of CDs to UV irradiation (365 nm) for intervals up to 7 h resulted in minimal fluorescence variation (Fig. S1B), underscoring the robust photostability of CDs.

CDs displayed a quick fluorescence decrease when exposed to AR, as shown in Fig. S2A. A sequence of CDs solutions with varying AR concentrations were prepared and their fluorescence at 395 nm was monitored. A decreasing trend in fluorescence intensity matched the increase in AR levels up to 500 μM, where almost complete quenching was noticed (Fig. 2C). The plot showing the CDs emission versus AR concentration ([AR]) followed the equation I0/I-1 = 0.01896 [AR] + 0.94645, with a high correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.992), where I0 is the fluorescence without AR and I is with AR present (Fig. 2D). This yielded a calculated LOD for AR of 5.9 μM. Subsequent to introducing AR concentrations varying from 0 to 200 μM, the UV–vis absorption profile of the 200 µg/mL CDs solution was recorded. A new absorption peak became apparent at 515 nm, intensifying as AR concentration augmented (Fig. 2E). The absorbance showed a direct linear correlation to AR levels within the 10 to 100 μM range, described by the equation A-A0 = 0.00287 [AR] + 0.00248, with R2 = 0.998, where A and A0 are the absorbance with and without AR, respectively.The computed LOD for AR was 0.53 μM (Fig. 2F).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The selectivity and anti-interference of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The discriminatory ability and immunity to disruption of the CDs probe were thoroughly assessed by introducing diverse interferents into the CDs mixture. Such discernment and the capacity to resist interference are crucial for practical applications. Introducing varying compounds led to the specific suppression of the CDs' luminescence only when AR was present, highlighting the probe's exceptional selectiveness toward AR. Moreover, adding different compounds to the CDs-AR mix left the fluorescence unchanged, confirming the selectivity of CDs in detecting AR, even in the presence of other substances (Fig. S2B).

After adding AR to the CDs solution, a new peak at 515 nm appeared in the absorption spectrum (Fig. 3 A), suggesting static quenching of fluorescence. Furthermore, the overlap of the CDs' fluorescence and AR's absorption spectra seen in Fig. 3B indicates that Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) could be involved in quenching. Fluorescence decay time of CDs, measured previously and afterwards AR insertion, was 4.2 and 3.89 ns, correspondingly (Fig. 3C), which allowed for the calculation of the donor–acceptor distance (γ) using Eqs. (1)–(3) [49]. The determined γ of 20.37 Å between CDs and AR aligns with the criteria for FRET (Fig. 3C) [50].
(1) E = 1-τ0/τ
(2) R0 = 0.211[κ2n-4ΦxJ(λ)]1/6 (in Å unit)
(3) E = 1/[1+(γ/R0)6] (in Å unit)

The baseline fluorescence lifetime of CDs, noted as τ0, pertains to conditions absent of AR, whereas τ denotes the lifetime when AR is included. Typically, the orientation parameter for the transition dipoles in the donor and acceptor, labeled κ2, is assumed to hold a value of 2/3. The symbol Φ represents the CDs' quantum efficiency, with n indicating the refractive index of the environment, and J(λ) is the overlap integral from the combination of the donor emission and acceptor absorption spectra. Fig. 3D shows the sensing method of CDs targeting AR, incorporating both static quenching and FRET mechanisms.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting AR in the real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To additionally assess the practical applicability of CDs, we performed quantitative determinations of AR in tap water, lemon-flavored vitamin water, and grapefruit juice. The accuracy of the CDs-based fluorescent probe was validated using the standard addition method [39]. Analytical results are presented in Table 1 . Recovered AR concentrations in these real water samples varied from 97.4 to 102.4 %, while relative standard deviations (RSD) below 0.33 %. These findings confirm the CDs probe's reliability for AR measurement in real-world water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AR in vegetable and beverages with a smartphone platform
ce:section.ce:para: Observations from Fig. 4 A revealed that upon introducing AR to CDs solution, a discernible color change from light yellow to light pink occurred, which intensified as AR concentration increased. This visual transition allowed for the direct observation of colorimetric changes with the naked eye. To capture more nuanced color shifts, we harnessed the capabilities of a smartphone to develop a reliable, real-time detection tool. The smartphone system recorded the red (R) and green (G) values from the images, establishing a linear correlation between the R/G ratio and AR concentration ([AR]) shown in Fig. 4B. Within the 0–100 μM range, the relationship was defined as R/G = 0.00484 [AR] + 0.98392, with an R2 of 0.996. The LOD was established to be 0.05 μM. This novel approach was benchmarked against previous literature, as summarized in Table 2 , and displayed comparable or superior performance. These outcomes affirm the CDs probe's potential for in situ and online monitoring of AR, highlighting its exceptional selectivity and sensitivity.

Recognizing that beverages are a ubiquitous part of daily life, we opted to analyze a beverage sample to detect AR utilizing the RGB method. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the incorporation of AR into the CDs-beverage system induced a concentration-dependent color transition from light yellow to pink. Subsequently, the smartphone was used to record the R and G intensity values from the images, enabling the establishment of a direct relationship between the R/G ratio and the levels of AR, as displayed in Fig. 5B. Within the 0–100 μM concentration range, the relationship was linear and described by the equation R/G = 0.01172 [AR] + 0.99014, with an R2 of 0.998. The LOD was 0.68 μM.

Motivated by the exceptional detection capabilities of CDs, flammulina velutipes, commonly known as needle mushrooms, were chosen to demonstrate the practical application of CDs in real sample analysis. As illustrated in Fig. 5C, the mushrooms remained white after immersion in a 200 μg/mL CDs solution for 2 h. Subsequently, the mushrooms were immersed in AR solutions (0–300 μM) and incubated for 12 h, resulting in a gradual shift in color from white to pink. Utilizing smartphone software to evaluate the corresponding RGB values, we established a robust linear correlation between the R/G ratio and AR the concentration as defined by the equation R/G = 0.0013 [AR] + 0.98658 with an R2 of 0.9997. The LOD was found to be 4.8 μM (Fig. 5D).

In conclusion, we synthesized a nanoprobe based on CDs through the method of pyrolysis, reaching a production yield of 47%. The CDs nanoprobe, which can perform selective fluorometric and spectrophotometric detection of AR, is characterized by rapid fluorescence suppression, development and intensification of an absorption band at 515 nm. Additionally, with rising AR levels, the CDs + AR solution transitions from light yellow to pink, enabling colorimetric detection of AR. Leveraging these color changes, we designed an affordable, portable smartphone-based sensing platform capable of quantitatively tracking AR in actual water samples, drinks and flammulina mushrooms. The CDs-based fluorescent sensor, alongside the portable smartphone platform, was been successfully implemented for visual in-situ monitoring of AR in beverages and vegetables, demonstrating satisfactory recoveries and precise measurements. This versatile nanoprobe, featuring a three-mode readout capability, and shows considerable potential for tracking hazardous materials in the context of food security and environmental surveillance.
Xueyu Fu: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Min Zheng: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51873023), the Research Project of Science and Technology of the Education Department of Jilin Province (No. JJKH20250699KJ).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.114030.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Coal bottom ash derived zeolite (SSZ-13) for the sorption of synthetic anion Alizarin Red S (ARS) dye
J. Hazard. Mater.

Anal. Lett.

Chem. Eng. Process.-Process Intens.

Ionics

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dual-analyte electrochemical sensor for fructose and alizarin red S specifically sensitive detection based on indicator displacement assay
Electrochim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu, F., & Kan, X. (2019). Dual-analyte electrochemical sensor for fructose and alizarin red S specifically sensitive detection based on indicator displacement assay. Electrochimica Acta, 319, 286-292.

Dyes Pigm.

Spectrochim. Acta Part A: Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy, 321, 124708.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lysosome targeting carbon dots-based fluorescent probe for monitoring pH changes in vitro and in vivo
